You are working on the pinterest/ktlint repository.
The codebase is at version 0.49 (commit: c5ec2c6a).

## Issue to Resolve

Spacing around `..<` should be identical to spacing around `..`
Given code below with inconsistent spacing around `..<`:
```
@OptIn(ExperimentalStdlibApi::class)
fun foo(int: Int): String =
    when (int) {
        in 10..<20 -> "A"
        in 20..< 30 -> "B"
        in 30 ..< 40 -> "C"
        in 40 ..<50 -> "D"
        else -> "unknown"
    }
```

should be formatted as:
```
@OptIn(ExperimentalStdlibApi::class)
fun foo(int: Int): String =
    when (int) {
        in 10..<20 -> "A"
        in 20..<30 -> "B"
        in 30..<40 -> "C"
        in 40..<50 -> "D"
        else -> "unknown"
    }
```

## Relevant Files

The following files are likely relevant to solving this issue:
- `CHANGELOG.md`
- `ktlint-rule-engine-core/src/main/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/rule/engine/core/api/ElementType.kt`
- `ktlint-ruleset-standard/src/main/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/ruleset/standard/rules/SpacingAroundRangeOperatorRule.kt`

## Instructions

Please analyze this issue and implement a fix. Make the necessary code changes to resolve the problem described above.

content.text:
The changes are complete. Here's a summary of what was done:

## Changes Made

### 1. `ktlint-rule-engine-core/src/main/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/rule/engine/core/api/ElementType.kt`
- Added `RANGE_UNTIL` element type mapping to `KtTokens.RANGE_UNTIL` (line 216), right after the existing `RANGE` entry. This exposes the `..<` operator token type that already exists in Kotlin's compiler.

### 2. `ktlint-ruleset-standard/src/main/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/ruleset/standard/rules/SpacingAroundRangeOperatorRule.kt`
- Added import for `RANGE_UNTIL`
- Extended the condition from `node.elementType == RANGE` to `node.elementType == RANGE || node.elementType == RANGE_UNTIL` so the rule also applies to the `..<` operator
- Changed hardcoded `".."` strings in error messages to use `node.text` dynamically, so the messages correctly show `".."` or `"..<"` depending on which operator was encountered
